{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[Singapore]]. Built by the [[Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[World War II]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]] {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Japanese |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical Built by the [[Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[World War II]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Japanese Built by the [[Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[World War II]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[Singapore]]. Built by the [[Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[World War II]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Japanese revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[Singapore]]. Built by the [[Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[World War II]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1942]] [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Syonan Shrine]] [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons category|Syonan Jinja}} [[ja:昭南神社]] [[zh:昭南神社]]

image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[Singapore]]. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[World War II]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1942]] [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] }} [[sn:Syonan Shrine]] [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons category|Syonan Jinja}} [[ja:昭南神社]] [[zh:昭南神社]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[Singapore]]. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[:en:Japanese occupation of Singapore|Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[:en:National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinto shrine}} ]] {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1942]] [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] }} [[sn:Syonan Shrine]] [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons revision.comment: Text replacement - "{{Shinto shrine}}" to "<!--this was an inappropriate and erroneous transclusion meant to be a bottom bar thing I think but not including-->"
revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q7662965]] Wikidata name: Syonan Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[:en:Singapore|Singapore]]. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[:en:Japanese occupation of Singapore|Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[:en:National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1942]] [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] }} [[sn:Syonan Shrine]] [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons category|Syonan Jinja}} [[ja:昭南神社]] [[zh:昭南神社]]

The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[sn:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q7662965]] Wikidata name: Syonan Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[:en:Singapore|Singapore]]. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[:en:Japanese occupation of Singapore|Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[:en:National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1942]] [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] }} [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons category|Syonan Jinja}} [[ja:昭南神社]] [[zh:昭南神社]] {{wikidata link|Q7662965}}

Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[:en:Singapore|Singapore]]. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[:en:Japanese occupation of Singapore|Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[:en:National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[:en:Singapore|Singapore]]. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[:en:Japanese occupation of Singapore|Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[:en:National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[sn:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7662965]] Wikidata name: Syonan Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrine]] | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]], [[:en:Singapore|Singapore]]. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the [[:en:Japanese occupation of Singapore|Japanese occupation of Singapore]] in [[:en:Pacific War|Pacific War]], the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), the [[:en:National Heritage Board (Singapore)|National Heritage Board]] (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the [[:en:Central Catchment Nature Reserve|Central Catchment Nature Reserve]] (CCNR), in which includes the [[:en:MacRitchie Reservoir|MacRitchie Reservoir]]. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinmei shrines}} {{draft categories| }} [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons category|Syonan Jinja}} [[ja:昭南神社]] revision.comment: Text replacement - "[[Category:Pages without translation templates]]" to "[[Category:qq]]"
revision.text:
[[az:Şonan məbədi]][[co:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[co:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[sn:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7662965]] Wikidata name: Syonan Jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:38, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = {{ill|MacRitchie Reservoir|en|MacRitchie Reservoir|WD=Q7331703|lt=MacRitchie Reservoir}}, Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at {{ill|MacRitchie Reservoir|en|MacRitchie Reservoir|WD=Q7331703|lt=MacRitchie Reservoir}}, {{ill|Singapore|en|Singapore|WD=Q334|lt=Singapore}}. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the {{ill|Japanese occupation of Singapore|en|Japanese occupation of Singapore|WD=Q715645|lt=Japanese occupation of Singapore}} in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the {{ill|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|en|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|WD=Q5060520|lt=Central Catchment Nature Reserve}} (CCNR), the {{ill|National Heritage Board (Singapore)|en|National Heritage Board (Singapore)|WD=Q6973045|lt=National Heritage Board}} (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the {{ill|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|en|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|WD=Q5060520|lt=Central Catchment Nature Reserve}} (CCNR), in which includes the {{ill|MacRitchie Reservoir|en|MacRitchie Reservoir|WD=Q7331703|lt=MacRitchie Reservoir}}. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons category|Syonan Jinja}} [[ja:昭南神社]] [[zh:昭南神社]] {{wikidata link|Q7662965}} [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1942]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the {{ill|Japanese occupation of Singapore|en|Japanese occupation of Singapore|WD=Q715645|lt=Japanese occupation of Singapore}} in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the {{ill|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|en|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|WD=Q5060520|lt=Central Catchment Nature Reserve}} (CCNR), the {{ill|National Heritage Board (Singapore)|en|National Heritage Board (Singapore)|WD=Q6973045|lt=National Heritage Board}} (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. revision.comment: imported only latest revision from https://archive.org/details/miraheze-wiki-shintowiki-20251218 to avoid excessive disk usage, comment on the revision was Removing {{afc comment}} templates
revision.text:
[[az:Şonan məbədi]][[co:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[co:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]][[sn:commons:Category:Syonan Jinja]][[en:Syonan Shrine]][[ja:昭南神社]][[zh:昭南神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Singapore}} {{infobox religious building | name = Syonan Jinja
Shōnan Shrine
昭南神社 | image = Shinto shrine in Shonan (Singapore) - 194210.jpg | caption = The near-completed Syonan Jinja in 1942 | map_type = Singapore | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|1|20|53.6|N|103|48|49.7|E|type:landmark|display=inline, title}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = {{ill|Shinto shrine|en|Shinto shrine|WD=Q845945|lt=Shinto shrine}} | deity = [[Amaterasu]] | founded_by = | established = {{start date|1942|05|07}} | date_destroyed = {{end date|1945|08|15}} | location = {{ill|MacRitchie Reservoir|en|MacRitchie Reservoir|WD=Q7331703|lt=MacRitchie Reservoir}}, Singapore | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{Nihongo|'''Syonan Jinja'''|昭南神社|Shōnan Jinja|lit. "Shōnan Shrine"}} was a [[Shinto]] shrine at {{ill|MacRitchie Reservoir|en|MacRitchie Reservoir|WD=Q7331703|lt=MacRitchie Reservoir}}, {{ill|Singapore|en|Singapore|WD=Q334|lt=Singapore}}. Built by the [[:en:Japanese Imperial Army|Japanese Imperial Army]] during the {{ill|Japanese occupation of Singapore|en|Japanese occupation of Singapore|WD=Q715645|lt=Japanese occupation of Singapore}} in {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|WD=Q184425|lt=Pacific War}}, the shrine was destroyed directly before British forces re-occupied Singapore.{{cite web |last1=Heok |first1=Tay Jin |title=Syonan Jinja: Singapore's Hidden Shrine That Is The Grandest After The Meiji Shrine In Tokyo |url=https://thesmartlocal.com/read/syonan-jinja/ |website=TheSmartLocal |access-date=19 April 2022 |date=24 August 2021}} Located deep inside the {{ill|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|en|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|WD=Q5060520|lt=Central Catchment Nature Reserve}} (CCNR), the {{ill|National Heritage Board (Singapore)|en|National Heritage Board (Singapore)|WD=Q6973045|lt=National Heritage Board}} (NHB) declared the site a historic site in 2002, although no plans have been made public so far to develop or make any significant changes to the site since the war.{{cite web |last1=Yap |first1=Gracia |title=Fast facts about the Syonan Jinja |url=https://tnp.straitstimes.com/news/singapore/fast-facts-about-syonan-jinja |website=The New Paper |access-date=19 April 2022 |language=en |date=2 November 2020}} == History == The Syonan Jinja (Light of the South Shrine) was envisioned to be a shrine that commemorated the many Japanese soldiers and military personnel who fell in the [[:en:Battle of Singapore|Japanese conquest of Singapore]]. The construction of Syonan Jinja was significant. Major Yasuji Tamura, the commander of the Japanese 5th Division's Engineers Regiment and the man in-charge of the design and the construction of the Syonan Jinja, had envisioned for the shrine in Singapore to be best Shinto shrine in the whole of the southern areas of Asia that has come under Japanese occupation. It was also to be the second-greatest shrine of the Shinto faith after the [[Meiji Jingu|Meiji Shrine]] in Tokyo, Japan. == Destruction == Just before the Japanese officially surrendered on 15 August in 1945, they decided to destroy and burn the shrine to the ground in fear of its desecration by the returning British colonial forces. == Preservation == The ruins of Syonan Jinja – now down to a few remaining support structures and broken pieces and chunks – can still be found in the dense tropical forest of the {{ill|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|en|Central Catchment Nature Reserve|WD=Q5060520|lt=Central Catchment Nature Reserve}} (CCNR), in which includes the {{ill|MacRitchie Reservoir|en|MacRitchie Reservoir|WD=Q7331703|lt=MacRitchie Reservoir}}. The remnants of the old shrine were designated as a historical site by the NHB in 2002.{{cite web |url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |title=Syonan Jinja on Infopedia |work=National Library Board |date=2004-12-24 |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151002032352/http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/infopedia/articles/SIP_236_2004-12-24.html |archive-date=2015-10-02 |url-status=live }} However, the historical site remains inaccessible to the public due to the strong current deep stream and the presence of wild animals.{{cite web |url=http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |title=Syonan Jinja |work=National Heritage Board |date= |access-date=2015-08-11 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20151023021744/http://www.nhb.gov.sg/places/historic-sites/syonan-jinja |archive-date=2015-10-23 |url-status=dead }} == References == {{Reflist}} ==Gallery== image:昭南神社-陸海将星の参拝.jpg|Japanese army and navy officers visiting the Shonan Jinja in 1943 image:昭南神社邮票（日治马来）.jpg|Japanese postage stamp depicting Shonan Jinja == External links == [https://remembersingapore.org/2017/07/16/macritchie-reservoir-syonan-jinja/ Exploring the Ruins of Syonan Jinja at MacRitchie Reservoir] {{portalbar|Japan|Singapore}} {{Shinmei shrines}} [[en:Syonan Shrine]] [[az:Şonan məbədi]] {{Commons category|Syonan Jinja}} [[ja:昭南神社]] [[zh:昭南神社]] {{wikidata link|Q7662965}} [[Category:1942 establishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:1945 disestablishments in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:20th-century architecture in Singapore]] [[Category:20th-century Shinto shrines]] [[Category:Japanese occupation of Singapore]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in 1942]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures in Singapore]] [[Category:Shinmei shrines]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in the Japanese colonial empire]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]
